title: Synthesis of Copper(II) Sulfate
purpose: To synthesize copper sulfate crystals through a neutralization reaction between a metal carbonate and an acid.
real_world_application: Copper sulfate is widely used in agriculture as a fungicide (Bordeaux mixture), in swimming pools to control algae, and in educational laboratories to demonstrate crystallization and chemical reactions.
safety: Wear safety goggles and gloves throughout the experiment.
Sulfuric acid is corrosive; handle with extreme care.
Copper carbonate is harmful if swallowed; do not ingest.
Be careful when heating the acid; do not let it boil vigorously.

steps.instruction: Step 1: Measurement. Carefully measure 25 mL of dilute sulfuric acid into a beaker. Hold the beaker labeled 'Acid' (number 1) in front of the camera to verify.

Step 2: Mixing & Reaction. Add small amounts of copper carbonate powder to the acid while stirring with a glass rod. You will see effervescence as carbon dioxide is released. Hold the stirring rod (number 2) to continue.

Step 3: Neutralization. Continue adding copper carbonate until no more bubbles are produced and a small amount of green powder remains at the bottom. This indicates the acid is fully neutralized. Show the beaker (number 3) to the camera.

Step 4: Filtration. Filter the blue solution into an evaporating dish to remove the excess copper carbonate. The clear blue liquid is your copper sulfate solution. Show the evaporating dish (number 4) to finish.
